title: Model Predictive Path Integral PID Control for Learning-Based Path Following
abstract: 经典的比例-积分-微分（PID）控制广泛应用于工业领域，但追求更高性能往往促使人们采用模型预测控制（MPC）。尽管基于梯度的方法是实时优化的标准方案，但基于采样的方法近年来备受关注。特别是模型预测路径积分（MPPI）控制能够实现无需梯度的优化，并兼容不可微模型与目标函数。然而，直接对控制输入序列进行采样可能导致输入不连续，且优化维度会随预测时域线性增加。本研究提出MPPI-PID控制方法，通过在每个控制步骤应用MPPI优化PID增益，从而用低维增益空间优化替代直接的高维输入序列优化。该框架通过PID结构提升了采样效率并生成更平滑的控制输入。我们还提供了理论分析，包括统一MPPI与MPPI-PID的信息论阐释、优化维度对采样效率影响的分析，以及PID结构对输入连续性的影响机制。所提方法在基于学习的微型叉车路径跟踪任务中进行了验证，采用融合物理模型与神经网络的残差学习动力学模型，并利用真实驾驶数据完成系统辨识。数值路径跟踪实验表明：相较于固定增益PID控制，MPPI-PID显著提升了跟踪性能；与传统MPPI控制相比，在实现相当性能的同时大幅降低了输入增量。此外，即使在采样数量显著减少的情况下，该方法仍能保持优越性能，证明了其更高的采样效率。

Model Predictive Path Integral PID Control for Learning-Based Path Following
Teruki Kato
teruki.kato.tg@mosk.tytlabs.co.jp
Koshi Oishi
Seigo Ito
Abstract
Classical proportional–integral–derivative (PID) control is widely employed in industrial applications; however, achieving higher performance often motivates the adoption of model predictive control (MPC).
Although gradient-based methods are the standard for real-time optimization, sampling-based approaches have recently gained attention.
In particular, model predictive path integral (MPPI) control enables gradient-free optimization and accommodates non-differentiable models and objective functions.
However, directly sampling control input sequences may yield discontinuous inputs and increase the optimization dimensionality in proportion to the prediction horizon.
This study proposes MPPI–PID control, which applies MPPI to optimize PID gains at each control step, thereby replacing direct high-dimensional input-sequence optimization with low-dimensional gain-space optimization.
This formulation enhances sample efficiency and yields smoother inputs via the PID structure.
We also provide theoretical insights, including an information-theoretic interpretation that unifies MPPI and MPPI–PID, an analysis of the effect of optimization dimensionality on sample efficiency, and a characterization of input continuity induced by the PID structure.
The proposed method is evaluated on the learning-based path following of a mini forklift using a residual-learning dynamics model that integrates a physical model with a neural network.
System identification is performed with real driving data. Numerical path-following experiments demonstrate that MPPI–PID improves tracking performance compared with fixed-gain PID and achieves performance comparable to conventional MPPI while significantly reducing input increments.
Furthermore, the proposed method maintains favorable performance even with substantially fewer samples, demonstrating its improved sample efficiency.

Introduction
In practical control development, classical proportional–integral–derivative (PID) control remains the established standard.
However, to achieve control performance beyond that of PID control, model predictive control (MPC)
[
11
]
has been actively studied.
As computational methods for real-time optimization, gradient-based methods are standard, yet sampling-based approaches have recently attracted attention.
Model predictive path integral (MPPI) control
[
15
]
, a representative method in the latter category, provides a highly flexible framework for computing control inputs without gradient calculations, even when the plant model or objective function is non-differentiable.
This property enables MPPI to handle plant models based on complex nonlinear machine-learning approaches, such as neural networks
[
1
]
, as well as control specifications with objective functions involving logical branching
[
6
]
, providing substantial practical benefits.
Conversely, as MPPI determines control input based on random samples, the resulting input often exhibits discontinuities.
Furthermore, because MPPI directly optimizes the input sequence, it does not retain the standard PID-controller architecture that is widely adopted in industrial practice, which can complicate deployment and tuning.
In addition, (as with standard MPC) the dimensionality of the optimization variables scales with the horizon length, which imposes a substantial computational burden.
To realize MPC-based real-time optimization while preserving the structure and continuity of PID control, approaches have been proposed that treat PID gains—rather than the control input itself—as optimization variables and perform gradient-based real-time optimization
[
17
,
16
,
5
,
7
]
.
Various studies
[
17
,
16
,
5
]
have targeted linear systems, while others
[
7
]
have targeted nonlinear systems based on physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)
[
1
]
.
However, gradient-based optimization methods are often challenging to employ when the plant model or objective function is non-differentiable and require computationally expensive gradient calculations.
Therefore, in this study, we propose a method that updates PID gains—treated as optimization variables—at each control step via sampling-based, model-based optimization using MPPI.
The objective is to preserve the structure and continuity of PID control while ensuring versatility (for handling complex control objectives) and computational efficiency.
Specifically, the expected benefits are as follows.
1.
Handling complex control objectives: As the optimization is based on MPPI, the method can be applied even when the plant model or objective function is non-differentiable.
2.
Continuity of the control input: As the PID control law has a filter structure based on the integral and derivative of the error, the control input tends to be smoother than in conventional MPPI, which directly samples the control input.
3.
Computational efficiency: In sampling-based optimization using MPPI, computationally expensive gradient calculations are unnecessary.
Moreover, while in conventional MPPI the dimensionality of the optimization variables scales with the horizon length, in the proposed method, the dimensionality of the optimization variables equals the (small) number of PID gains, so improved sample efficiency is expected.
4.
Compatibility with existing PID controllers: By optimizing only the PID gains while preserving the PID control-law structure, the method can enhance performance without redesigning or replacing existing PID controllers commonly used in industrial applications.
Furthermore, we provide theoretical insights that elucidate the proposed method.
Specifically, we (i) present an information-theoretic interpretation of an update rule that unifies conventional MPPI with the proposed method, (ii) analyze the influence of the optimization dimension on sample efficiency through the effective sample size, and (iii) analyze the continuity of the control input arising from the PID structure.
Among related methods, PI
2
(policy improvement with path integrals)
[
14
]
is a path-integral-type reinforcement learning approach closely related to MPPI.
PI
2
, which has also been applied to the tuning of PID gains, is a model-free method that optimizes control policy parameters on an episode-by-episode basis
[
2
]
.
In contrast, the proposed method employs model-based, receding-horizon optimization at each control step, thereby enabling online adaptation of the PID gains in response to predicted future tracking cost under the current operating condition.
In this study, we apply the proposed control method to a residual-learning model that integrates a physical model and a neural network (Fig.
1
).
Fig. 1:
Overview of the proposed method
In particular, as a framework for learning residuals relative to a known physical model via neural networks, we employ universal differential equations (UDEs)
[
10
]
, which are widely used in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML).
Building on this framework, we use a model that integrates two complementary approaches: one that learns only the dynamics of a subset of state dimensions
[
13
,
3
,
12
]
and another that adjusts the residual magnitude depending on the state
[
4
]
.
Furthermore, we apply the proposed method to system identification and path-following control using real driving data from a mini forklift platform, representing a typical industrial vehicle.
In particular, we benchmark the proposed control method against conventional fixed-gain PID control and standard MPPI, demonstrating improvements in path-following performance and continuity of control inputs, as well as in sample efficiency.

5
Conclusions
In this study, we introduce a receding-horizon PID gain optimization method based on MPPI (MPPI–PID).
We establish theoretical insights that unify conventional MPPI and MPPI–PID within an information-theoretic framework, and demonstrate, through effective-sample-size and temporal-correlation analyses, the sample-efficiency and input-smoothness advantages of the proposed approach.
The method is applied to learning-based forklift path-following control using a residual-learning dynamics model trained on real-machine data.
Numerical results show improved tracking performance compared with fixed-gain PID control and smoother inputs than conventional MPPI, while retaining favorable performance under substantially reduced sample counts.
Future work includes experimental validation on a real machine, extension to stochastic control under model uncertainty, and integration with obstacle-avoidance strategies.

preview_text:
Classical proportional--integral--derivative (PID) control is widely employed in industrial applications; however, achieving higher performance often motivates the adoption of model predictive control (MPC). Although gradient-based methods are the standard for real-time optimization, sampling-based approaches have recently gained attention. In particular, model predictive path integral (MPPI) control enables gradient-free optimization and accommodates non-differentiable models and objective functions. However, directly sampling control input sequences may yield discontinuous inputs and increase the optimization dimensionality in proportion to the prediction horizon. This study proposes MPPI--PID control, which applies MPPI to optimize PID gains at each control step, thereby replacing direct high-dimensional input-sequence optimization with low-dimensional gain-space optimization. This formulation enhances sample efficiency and yields smoother inputs via the PID structure. We also provide theoretical insights, including an information-theoretic interpretation that unifies MPPI and MPPI--PID, an analysis of the effect of optimization dimensionality on sample efficiency, and a characterization of input continuity induced by the PID structure. The proposed method is evaluated on the learning-based path following of a mini forklift using a residual-learning dynamics model that integrates a physical model with a neural network. System identification is performed with real driving data. Numerical path-following experiments demonstrate that MPPI--PID improves tracking performance compared with fixed-gain PID and achieves performance comparable to conventional MPPI while significantly reducing input increments. Furthermore, the proposed method maintains favorable performance even with substantially fewer samples, demonstrating its improved sample efficiency.

Model Predictive Path Integral PID Control for Learning-Based Path Following
